title: Quiz: Sampling and Inference in QBMs
description: Test your understanding of quantum sampling and inference techniques

questions.question: What is the probability of measuring computational basis state |z⟩ from a quantum Gibbs state?
questions.explanation: For a density matrix ρ, the probability of measuring basis state |z⟩ is P(z) = ⟨z|ρ|z⟩, which is the z-th diagonal element of ρ.

questions.question: In quantum annealing for sampling, what is the purpose of the driver Hamiltonian H₀?

options.text: To encode the problem to be solved
To introduce quantum fluctuations that enable tunneling

questions.explanation: The driver Hamiltonian (typically a transverse field) introduces quantum fluctuations that allow the system to tunnel between states, enabling exploration of the energy landscape.

questions.question: How is conditional inference performed in a QBM given observed variables v_obs?

options.text: By setting observed qubits to random states
By projecting the Gibbs state onto the observed configuration

questions.explanation: Conditional inference is performed by applying a projector onto the observed configuration: ρ_cond = Π_obs ρ Π_obs / Tr[Π_obs ρ Π_obs], which gives the conditional distribution.

questions.question: What advantage can quantum-enhanced MCMC potentially provide?

options.text: Reduced mixing time for certain distributions
Elimination of the need for burn-in

questions.explanation: Quantum dynamics can potentially reduce mixing time by enabling tunneling between local minima, which classical MCMC cannot easily traverse.

questions.question: Measuring a quantum Gibbs state in the X-basis instead of the Z-basis can reveal information about quantum coherence.
questions.explanation: True. Measuring in different bases (X, Y) reveals off-diagonal elements of the density matrix in the computational basis, which represent quantum coherence.

questions.question: What is the mixing time in the context of MCMC sampling?

options.text: The time to generate one sample
The number of steps until the chain reaches the stationary distribution
The total runtime of the algorithm

questions.explanation: Mixing time measures how many steps the Markov chain needs to converge to (or get close to) its stationary distribution, regardless of the starting state.

questions.question: In reverse annealing, what is the starting point?

options.text: The ground state of the driver Hamiltonian

questions.explanation: Reverse annealing starts from a known classical configuration, then introduces quantum fluctuations to explore nearby states before annealing back to refine the solution.

questions.question: Which applications can benefit from QBM inference? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: QBM inference supports (a) image completion via conditional sampling, (b) anomaly detection by evaluating data likelihoods, and (c) feature extraction using hidden unit activations. Polynomial factorization is not a probabilistic inference task.
